speaker: interviewer
text: Tape Three, interview with Ellen Leviatin Kaidanow-- Kaidanow, September 20th, 1995. He did not know you were Jewish. He thought you were her illegitimate child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly, I think that he knew but either he liked me, or he didn't care. Or it was my luck to remain alive. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody said to me that he knew. No, they had no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And about how old were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anya ?] was the youngest sister of Lena.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how to judge her age. She was a very beautiful woman. I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also remember that she was going out with the German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Along being-- with-- married, she went out with German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he was mostly away, in the-- the rafters. I don't know where they were keeping themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- you know, I-- I-- I just-- something with an M. I think it was Micheal or--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- was he-- he was Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work for the Germans? Or was he just--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the Ukrainian police.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Ukrainian police, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And the Ukrainian police collaborated with the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they would point out people that were in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if they knew they were Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: --they were the ones that point them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did [? Anya ?] know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- and she went out with German officers?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she never let him know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Him, I remember, they used to--

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said to you, I had to hide when he drank.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they figured, you know, when you're drunk, you sometimes--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Lena? How did she handle him when he was drunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was just out of sight. But she didn't hide with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me was-- just not to be in his way, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Yeah. What did you do in the cellar when they had to hide you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just sat there. I don't really remember exactly why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did anybody come down? Did Lena come down to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night she would ring me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. How long did this last?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- we stayed with her till the end of the war. So let's say, the war ended, what? In our town they came in, in early '45. Because, you know, we are closer to the Russians, I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we must have been liberated either late '44 or early '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. In this time, you said that [? Anya ?] lived in a city.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the city. How big a city was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: How-- does it go by people? I don't know. Kilometers? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Was it a city with cars and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember cars. But I think it was, you know, quite--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't, any more, a-- a village.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really a city. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And did you ever meet other children? Did you ever go outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one time I went out to play without her permission. And there were some boys and-- I think mostly boys. And they started yelling at me, [? [POLISH], ?] which means Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they threw stones at me. Which to this day, I have a mark right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since that time, she said, my god, you know? She was-- I mean, she was like, beside herself. She wanted to go to the police--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, make a whole big deal out of it. Because how dare they. Because she was like, crazy about me. I mean, this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? We were attached to each other, she to me and I to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, we stopped-- they stopped her from doing that. Because who wants to go to the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: But from that time on, I was really more hidden. Because she was afraid, you know? But nothing came of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took care of the financial aspects of feeding you? Did [? Anya-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Something with laundry, it had something to do with laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So did Lena. And one time I-- I think they brought some things that-- from the-- from Jews that were killed. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow that's in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what kind of things? What kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things, like clothes and things like that. I remember they were looking through piles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm sure that in that town, there must have been Jews too, that were killed. I'm sure of it. I don't know the history of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. How far is that town from--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not far. I don't-- you know, kilometers. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple of hours? Two hours? Five hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, probably two hours or so, two and a half--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So it's probably about 100 miles?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And when they worked, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lie-- hidden in that cold bunker--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-- did-- I-- I believe so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lena, sometimes she was home and sometimes not. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in that cold bunker, did you have any kind of a routine of how you did things, what you did? What happens if you had to go to the bathroom? I-- I-- see, the bathrooms there, I think, were outside. You know, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I must have gone out. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, the only time that I was sick from the coldness or whatever, I had terrible-- like, warts. Not warts, like, with pus, my feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And what did they do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from-- that was, already, towards the end of the-- and what did they do? I think they just cleaned it. She used to wash my feet, you know, in a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a pail, you know? She did everything for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was like, the most spoiled hidden child that ever was. Because she was just so good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever-- they ever take you to a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never went. I never knew about a doctor, dentist. I-- I'd never-- until I came to the United States, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever-- were you ever punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody would dare to touch me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was like my-- she was like, I-- I don't know. I guess she was my protector. I don't know. Nobody-- maybe I was-- for what were they going to punish me? I didn't do any-- you know what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- I don't remember ever doing anything except [? in ?] that [? whole ?] [? thought ?] that I told you, that you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the cows and butter and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how do you remember like, your days there, other than sitting in a cellar?

speaker: interviewer
text: If they were sorting clothing, did you help them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just remember seeing it and knowing what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how, what. I knew. I guess they must have said something, you know? Between them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever-- did Lena ever talk to you about your parents, your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that she wanted to do is talk Jewish to me at night. And I didn't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to be Jewish. I didn't want to hear anything Jewish. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you consider yourself Christian at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I didn't know religiously, Christian. You know what I mean? But I sort of became Lena's daughter, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. Did you go to church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was, already, towards the end, when we weren't so afraid. Because we knew, already, the war was coming and the Germans were retreating. So in fact, one of the pictures I think that you have, that I showed you, is in front of a church. I remember going to church. Is-- In fact, after we were liberated I went to church even more. And I remember-- I don't know, it must have-- I must have been there in December or something. Because I remember decorating the tree with them and being very excited about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already after the Germans left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did go to church. And in fact, after the liberation of this town-- it wasn't the end of the war. But we were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of young boys killed from mines, where they either stepped on it or picked it up. And I remember going to wakes and being very, very scared. Very, very scared. But I had to go. Because if all of them went and I was a Christian, I had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you always know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. But I didn't want to know. But I have to admit it, that I did know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall yourself asking questions--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or being conflicted about who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just probably thought that I'll spend the rest of-- I didn't think of the rest of my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I only knew that I-- I have her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's it. I didn't-- you know, I just knew that I had her. And in fact, as soon as our town was liberated, she sent me to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember liking it very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And putting on a red Pioneer-- I know they call it a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, it's a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not just a ribbon. It's like a kerchief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The communists, you know? And I remember loving school and really you know, being excited about it. The papers, and writing. It was very exciting to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't last very long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, figure it out. Let's say if it was-- I was five or six when it started. And now we're talking '44, '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? You add on the three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she registered you in what kind of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- like a public school, with other kids. And I just remember loving it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you know how to read and write?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I must have gone into with the first class or-- I don't even know how.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? I did not know how to read and write.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she registered you as her daughter?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And what happened with [? Anya ?] and her husband once the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He disappeared. I don't know if he got killed or ran away. Because he was afraid, as a Banderowiec--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever he did, that they'll be after him. And the three of us just lived in that same house.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a short time, by the way--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? Why-- why was it a short time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why it was a short time is this. After the liberation of our town, a landsman of ours-- in other words, somebody, a person from our town, wrote to this-- I think it was to the neighbor, I'm not even sure to whom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Czech neighbor. There was-- you know, from the very beginning--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I mentioned to you, we had a neighbor next door, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, she wrote to one of the people that survived. Is there anybody left from the family? The Leviatin family, or who's left from Dubno? And somebody wrote her back to Russia. She was in Russia at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That they think one of the-- the Leviatin children may be spared, alive with Lena.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came back to the town. And she started looking for me. And how she found out that I was not in Dubno, but rather than that Chervon Armeisk. I'll never know. But she came to our house. She kept asking and asking, and asking. And when she came she said to me-- because my given name was Shifra. She said Shifra. And I said, I'm not Shifra. I'm Marusia. And I ran away from her. And I went to school that day. And when I came back, she was there. And she decided to take me back with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in-- it's called Pervouralsk. It's like the-- I think Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: She may have been like a distant, distant relative.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened was, she wanted to take me back to her, to where she lived. And the only reason that Lena said she was going to give me up is because she felt I would have a better life with her. Like she could give me papers, and crayons, and whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I have to say, broken-heartedly, because for me to leave Lena was-- I think that I was more traumatized then than even-- because I was younger. And I was going with somebody that I really loved and knew. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did go back with her to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it explained to you by Lena?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you. She thought that I would have a better life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That she could give me. She said it to her, but I heard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I went back with her. We traveled you know, through Moscow. And I got there. It was very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. And did you answer to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was very-- for me, it was very traumatic. I mean, I just, you know, cried and cried. And so did Lena. You know? Because then we started to correspond.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that-- from that time on, I did stay with them. She, you know, her husband. And I don't know what year it was, whether it was totally after the war or what. But the Russians allowed Polish Jews to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever wanted to. So they had signed up to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know we traveled for a full month in the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just us. Many, many Jews that were Polish citizens, which we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that time on, we traveled together. From Russia, we stopped off in Lodz. And there we stayed, probably, a couple of nights. And you know, we were scared to stay there. Because even after the war, they still--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, people were killing Jews and groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, I believe we went to Czechoslovakia, as Greek-- as Greeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember crossing the border and not being allowed to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Well, you were illegally crossing the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supposed to be Greeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because apparently Greeks were allowed to go, and Jews were not. So you know, you live your whole life in fear. That's all I remember is fear, fear, fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Bricha, the Israeli-- the Haganah, or the Jewish Agency involved in getting you across these borders? From--

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe they must have been.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this was organized. It wasn't just us alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like, a whole caravan of people--

speaker: interviewee
text: --crossing, you know, into Czechoslovakia. In Czechoslovakia, we stayed a very short time. And then we came to the DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. I'd like to just go back to your-- the people who took you to-- to Siberia or the Ural. Did you give me their names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you-- what-- what were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [? Jenny ?] and [? Rubin ?] [? Greenberg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know whether they were relatives or they weren't?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they were, it was distant-- I mean, I-- I never really asked them what they--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was younger, 3 and 1/2 years younger, I think, than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And what was their motive in coming for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I think they really-- originally she came to see the-- they had property and things like that. I think she came to see her house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And things like that. And then she found out that I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And were-- how were they with you? How-- how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very good with me. But I think like, I wasn't good. You know? In other words, they were wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I guess I missed Lena, or something. That I just wasn't the same person. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When did you find out what happened to your parents and your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, this was much, much later. I don't know if it was in Germany or in the United States, that somebody came and said that they were hidden in the bunker. They ran out of food. My mother, as you can see in the picture, was a heavy woman. She was-- somebody said-- as thin as a stick. They were starving. And they had to get out of the bunker to look for food. And when they did, they were caught and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Oh, they were all together though--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I have to live with for the rest of my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: To think that they were starving. And you know, they had to get out of the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So you got back with these-- with the [? Greenberg ?] family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were Shura. And what was your last name again? You were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of yourself, in that time, as sort of being Marusia? Do you ever mix these things up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- you know, just by knowing how many names I had, you could imagine that I'm mixed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from Shura, we went to Poland. I became-- because Shura is only a nickname in Russian. It's Alexandra.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask me why, but that's what it is. So in Poland, I became Ala.

speaker: interviewee
text: This over here, the Green-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, that's what I was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Is that how you get Ellen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. But that became in the United States. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you've really had a lot of names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. What happened when you got to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we lived like, in a tremendous, beautiful house, you know, for a couple of weeks, with other Jews, all together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It must have been like a Czech mansion or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was a very short time. I don't know how many weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there we came to a German DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. You were with [? Jenny ?] and Rubin--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their-- what was their son's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Victor. And you were with them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if they took me in 1945--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came to Germany, Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we were a couple of months. And they registered us to a orphanage in Germany. It was called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They registered their child in an orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Just, you know, me and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Um-- I don't know, you know, whether or not he was their child or the same relationship as me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of you were registered as orphans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that they had any particular motive in taking care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they had a motive. Just that they loved children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. But what happened was, at that time, the United States had quotas. And they wanted to come to the United States, but couldn't. So they figured, at least let the children go. And we had, here, an aunt in New York, actually in Freeport, Long Island, to whom we were supposed to come. So we stayed like, a full year at that camp, [INAUDIBLE]. And-- It was a camp or an orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a camp. It was like a-- I guess for orphans. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, every time they got, let's say, a quota of people, they used to send out the orphans.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you didn't have who to come to, they placed you. Like, you know, I had a friend. She was placed in St Paul's. But we, officially, were going to this aunt. Her name was Edith Prafin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And she was in Freeport, Long Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: At least, that's what we thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: But unfortunately, when we got to the United States--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we came here by boat, and through this organization. We found out that she died three weeks before we arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a 16-year-old daughter, Bernice. And her husband. They had a candy store. It's ironic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had a candy store--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Long Island. You know? And so this daughter became our mother. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She signed us out from the organization--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was married. So she could--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a young girl that just lost her mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but they lived in a little house in Freeport. And you know, they had a candy store in Freeport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she had a father though.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had-- the father was alive, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were with her, like, for about eight months. And then the [? Greenbergs ?] came to the United States. So this, her father, arranged for us to have an apartment in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by that time, we became sort of a family again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school. You graduated here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did go to-- I graduated high school here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I'd like to get back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was just going to say that my first schooling that I had was, of course, that I told you, the few months--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with Lena. And then, basically, the main thing was really in Bad Reichenhall. Which we were there approximately a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Hebrew schools. Because they organize schools there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned a little bit Hebrew and writing. And that was my beginning of education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. And what kind of schools were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they were through the organizations. I don't know whether it was the UJA or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I think HIAS. I think maybe it was HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Was it Joint or HIAS?

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Israeli, the Palestinian organizations? Did they run anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Palestinian, I just remember belonging to a group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And what did they do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just, you know, for hiking--

speaker: interviewee
text: And trying to entice us to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which at one time I was considering to run away and go with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they wanted me to do actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't think I had the courage to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- in Bad Reichenhall-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you went to school. And then when you got moved to the orphanage, I presume you still stayed in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, they also-- they gave lessons like--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very little, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah. What language was used all along, in both Bad Reichenhall and in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember speaking-- you know, when I first came, I didn't speak Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it was because I put it in back of my mind--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and refused to speak. And I really didn't want to be Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did remember the language. Because it came back to me very quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I'm assuming that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak with the [? Greenbergs? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. Could you describe the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- it was two very beautiful places, there were two of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where they placed us. It was very nice, very beautiful. It was a common-- like a cafeteria-type eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was organized games. I remember playing ping pong and volleyball and going on boats. Because it was right on the [? Saa, ?] on the-- you know? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were having some good times there. You know, not bad but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had friends there. People that were in similar circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: But mostly, they were much older than I.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sort of like adopted me. In fact, with one girl, I'm still in touch. And she was like my mentor or something, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- like I was her little sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had dances there, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the older girls were flirting and dancing with the guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, mhm. Were-- was there any special attention given to the experiences that most of these kids had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody ever asked me. I never talked about it till now, to any-- even to my husband, in this kind of detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the school itself, the orphanage itself, was there any social services at all there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did kids relate to each other normally, in difficult ways, or in easy ways? Were they trusting?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I was always very sad. That, I know. But I know that the older ones enjoyed each other's company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. How about fears and dreams at night?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did-- did you ever discuss anything with kids? Or did anybody share their experiences with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with Lena at that point?

speaker: interviewer
text: You never spoke to-- wrote her at all once you left Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. OK. We're going to change our tape now.
